text: The surgeon begins the gastrectomy by mobilizing the stomach at the junction of the body and antrum. Dissection occurs between the gastroepiploic arcade and the stomach wall, preserving the right gastroepiploic artery and vein to maintain blood supply to the duodenum and proximal stomach.

The surgeon proceeds distally along the pylorus onto the duodenum, preserving the right gastroepiploic artery. Small twigs and branches from the gastroepiploic vessels are progressively divided as they extend onto the duodenal wall.

The surgeon releases the attachment of the anterior leaf of the mesocolon from the duodenum, achieving complete exposure of the duodenum at the point of duodenal transaction. The last few branches from the gastroepiploic vessels to the duodenum are divided, and the loose areolar tissue in the pancreaticoduodenal groove is dissected.
The surgeon navigates the pancreas duodenal groove, revealing the common hepatic lymph node at the upper border of the pancreas.

The surgeon focuses on the superior border of the duodenum, dividing the right gastric branches close to the duodenal wall. Smaller branches are divided using a harmonic scalpel, while larger branches are clipped before division. This process frees the superior border of the duodenum, preparing it for transaction.

The surgeon ligates the right gastric vessels close to the duodenal wall, and in cases of gastric adenopogastronoma, re-ligates them at their origin as part of the DII lymphadenectomy. The healthy, supple duodenum is identified as the site for potential duodenal transection, with no visible scarring.

The surgeon mobilizes the duodenum by dividing the gastrocolic omentum outside the gastroepiploic arcade. The anterior leaf of the mesocolon is released from the head of the pancreas and duodenum, and the gastroepiploic vessels are dissected down to their origin.

The surgeon achieves clear exposure of the head of the pancreas and the origin of the gastro-vibrioic vessels. The gastro-vibrioic artery and vein are doubly clipped and divided, completing the mobilization of the lower border of the duodenum.

The surgeon mobilizes the upper border of the duodenum and divides the right gastric vessels. The stumps of the right gastrovibular artery and vein are visible after transection. The inferior and posterior borders of the duodenum are cleared, preparing for duodenal transection. The stomach is transected, revealing a stricture extending more proximally into the distal body of the stomach than initially anticipated.

The surgeon observes the stricture extending more proximally in the stomach, prompting a decision to transect at a more proximal point in the mid body of the stomach. The surgical plan is adjusted from a Billroth-I to a Billroth-II anastomosis.

The surgeon mobilizes the greater curvature of the stomach along the gastro-bibloid arcade, preserving the arcade until reaching a healthy area for transaction. Concurrently, the lesser curvature is mobilized by dividing the cross-feed branches of the left gastric artery at the level of the incisura.

The surgeon encounters significant scarring at the lesser curve of the stomach during mobilization, which is associated with full-thickness stomach wall injury. This requires careful dissection to avoid complications.
The surgeon transects the duodenum using a purple reload stapler, ensuring a healthy duodenal stump is achieved. Following the transection, the stomach is lifted to continue the procedure.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the lesser curve of the stomach, addressing scarring at the site of the incisura to avoid bleeding. The mesentery at the lesser curve is divided into anterior and posterior laminae, with vessels in the posterior laminae being clipped and divided. This step follows the transection of the duodenum.

The surgeon prepares the stomach for transection at a more proximal, healthy, and non-strictured portion, taking the left gastric artery close to the wall near the staple line. The Ryle's tube is then withdrawn from the stomach.

The surgeon uses a green echelon reload stapler to transect the thick and edematous stomach, applying compression for several minutes to reduce edema before stapling. On the lesser curve side, a black reload stapler is used due to the assessed thickness, with similar compression applied to minimize bleeding from the staple line.

The surgeon inspects the gastric transection staple line for integrity and bleeding, focusing on the lesser curve side. A seromuscular stitch using 2-0 Vicryl is applied in a continuous fashion to reinforce the staple line and ensure hemostasis in the thick-walled stomach.

The surgeon opens the transverse mesocolon through an avascular window near the DJ flexure, identifying the DJ flexure and bringing the jejunal loop up in a tension-free manner for anastomosis. The procedure involves planning a Brown loop Bilroth II reconstruction for the gastrojejunostomy.

The surgeon selects a jejunal loop approximately fifteen to twenty centimeters from the DJ flexure for the gastrojejunostomy, ensuring space for a jejunojejunostomy as part of the Brown loop anastomosis. The stomach is devascularized at the site of the staple anastomosis to prevent interluminal bleeding, given the thick wall structure of the stomach.

The surgeon creates a small gastrotomy on the stomach, stretching it to accommodate a stapler. Air is insufflated into the thick-walled stomach to facilitate the procedure. Gastric contents are aspirated to prevent peritoneal spill during anastomosis. Two seromuscular hitching stitches are placed between the jejunum and stomach to align them for staple anastomosis.

A small entrotomy is made in the jejunum and stretched using a mirror lens or bubble grasper to accommodate forceps. The surgeon uses a purple tillot of Covidian for the gastrointestinal anastomosis, aligning the jejunal and gastric loops with sutures. After a two-minute compression, the staple is fired, and the staple line is inspected for hemostasis. The enterotomy is closed in a continuous fashion with absorbable sutures, starting at both corners and meeting at the center to prevent the distal corner from being hidden.

The surgeon completes the closure of the enterotomy by suturing the proximal corner first and then proceeding distally using a 3-0 Vicryl suture. A third layer of seromuscular suture is placed using 3-0 V-Loc, starting beyond the proximal point of the staple line to bring the stomach and jejunum together, avoiding tension on the staple line. The continuous seromuscular suture is then extended distally, reinforcing the anterior staple line and enterotomy closure.

The surgeon reinforces the anastomosis with a third layer of sutures, replicating open surgery techniques in a laparoscopic setting. The jejunojejunostomy is performed approximately eight to ten centimeters distal to the gastrojejunostomy as part of the Brown loop reconstruction.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn jejunojejunostomy as part of a Brown loop reconstruction, using a 2-0 Pedius suture in a continuous fashion. The enterotomy is made approximately three centimeters in length on the jejunum. The anterior layer of the anastomosis is completed with seromuscular and extra mucosal stitches, avoiding the mucosa to ensure good serosal approximation.

The surgeon completes the Billroth II reconstruction, including the gastro-jejunostomy and jejuno-jejunostomy, forming the brown-loop anastomosis. The duodenal staple line is inspected for integrity and hemostasis. Methylene blue is instilled through the reintroduced Ryle tube, visibly distending the bowel loops and confirming the integrity of both anastomoses with free flow into the proximal and distal limbs.

The surgeon performs lavage in the surgical area, closes the mesocolic window, and places a drain at the duodenal stump. The specimen is then retrieved through the suprapubic incision using a retrieval bag.
